comments.content: What is the difference between the sub-natural realm and the lower astral?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a complex question. The astral is the plane where most of our human thoughts and emotions exist, and so the subnatural realm contacts us through the lower astral, just as the supernatural realm contacts us through the upper astral. The theory, though, is that the subnatural -- like the supernatural -- is entirely outside of those planes that human beings can experience directly. The subnatural is denser, more restricting, more limited than the world we experience with our senses, just as the supernatural world is less dense, less restricted, less limiting than the highest and least material things we human beings can perceive.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: That's a good question to which there's no easy answer. The planes are simply the different modes through which we experience the cosmos -- the material plane is the cosmos as we experience it through our ordinary senses, the etheric plane the cosmos as we experience it through our sensitivity to the life force, and so on. The subnatural, natural, and supernatural realms, by contrast, are distinguished by scale -- we interact with the supernatural via the heavens in their vastness, and with the subnatural via the quantum realm. How do they relate to one another? I'm not yet sure.

replies.content:
This is a very interesting discussion—thank you all, and especially JMG!

In case it’s relevant—maybe, maybe not—in the book I’m currently writing about orlog, wyrd, and the Norns, one chapter covers what I call the quantum nature of the Norns’ work. Given JMG’s suggestion that the subnatural may equate to the quantum realm, it’s an interesting thought that by working through the subnatural realm, supernatural beings, the Norns, serve as a bridge between subnatural and supernatural, in the process shaping our human lives in the middle, the natural realm.

To briefly summarize the specifics, I’ll describe each Norn’s role in turn, as I see it.

Verdandi, the Norn of “What is Becoming”, of the very instant when potential becomes actual in the Midgard realm, works in the quantum field of wave function collapse. Her act of observing each being, event, phenomenon, as it comes into manifestation in Midgard serves as a catalyst for the process of Becoming. Her act of observation causes Schrödinger’s cat to collapse from a probabilistic state into a state of deadness or aliveness when the box is opened. Her supervision of Becoming also causes each thing that comes into being to take on its own Eigenstate, its individual characteristics, of which that being’s or event’s orlog or wyrd is a part. I see Verdandi, a supernatural being, as the midwife bringing about birth from the quantum realm—perhaps the subnatural realm—into what we call the natural realm of Midgard: very much a bridging function.

The name of the Norn Skuld comes from the word ‘shall, skulu, sceal’ but not primarily in its form as an auxiliary to a future tense. Rather, the old meaning of the word most often relates to obligation, debt, consequences, or necessity. Rather than implying a future tense, it generally implied a continuous present, something that is obliged to happen or should happen on a regular basis. I see Skuld’s role as recognizing and acting upon the debts, obligations, consequences that arise because of entanglements between all of us, all our deeds, relationships, actions, events, phenomena of Nature, everything. Quantum entanglement, and all entanglement, is the necessity that she responds to. What happens to one particle happens to all other particles that are entangled with it, creating complexities that she is obliged to deal with through orlog and wyrd.

Urdh or Wyrd is the Norn of “What-Is,” of everything that has happened, which continually creates and shapes the substrate for What-is-Becoming. What-is-Becoming arises from that substrate and is shaped by it, at the same time that each thing which Becomes reshapes the substance of What-Is. The effect and impact of the past upon each of us, and upon larger groups and cultures, depends a good deal upon our understanding and interpretation of the past as we know it. As you and many have discussed, JMG, all human knowledge is necessarily subjective, and yet we have ways to approximate an objective view. The past shapes the present in both subjective and objective ways.

For example, living in a country in a state of war, living through it, and continuing with life afterwards. There are / were many objective effects of this war, and many subjective effects, all of which were interpreted and reinterpreted in different ways, from different points of view, based on differing experiences and biases. These form part of the histories of the countries involved, which then shape aspects of their cultural identities. Later revisions of history may create significant changes of interpretation and thus different perspectives on identity and history.

This all relates to Urdh’s domain of What-Is: what *really* is, here? There is the objective Past, which we can never completely comprehend, and a multiplicity of subjective Pasts which would be impossible for us to understand in its entirety, and then there is all the reinterpretation that goes on, and all of this is impactful and relevant to us as humans, to Midgard, and to the Deities involved with humans and Midgard.

The way I try to philosophically sort my way through this confusion is by understanding Urdh’s work with What-Is as an expression of the quantum phenomenon of wave-particle duality, as in ‘light is both a particle and wave.’ Whether it manifests as a particle or a wave depends on the design of the experiment one is conducting to observe it. I liken the objective past to a particle, and the subjective past to a wave. There are more-objective ways to study the past, such as archaeology or geology (which still depend on subjective interpretations, but not entirely on them), and very subjective ways of studying the past such as clairvoyant work or ancestral-spirit contact. And there are a great many ways that lie in between those extremes, like various approaches to studying history or anthropology, or one’s own family history.

The What-Is that Urdh lays in layers of orlog in the Well of Wyrd is not only the objective past but also subjective past, because what she and all the Norns focus on is *significance* rather than strict factuality. Their shaping of orlog is based on meaning: the meaning that deeds and events have for them, for us, for the Gods, for Midgard’s wyrd as a whole. The substance of her weaving is both wave and particle, subjective and objective, and takes its meaning therefrom. This both / and nature of her weaving of reality partakes of the nature of the quantum realm as does the work of her sisters.

If, as you suggest, the subnatural realm consists of or includes the quantum realm, then I see the Norns as the midwives and mediators between subnatural, supernatural, and natural realms. And not incidentally, their weavings result in layers of orlog, wyrd, fate being laid in the Well and nourishing the World-Tree: the containers of Time and Space. This involvement of orlog in turn is relevant to what you’ve written in the articles you linked to your comment above, about the consequences of paths of evil, the quantum nature of cyberspace, etc.

As always, many thanks to you and the commentariat for these discussions!

Winifred Hodge Rose

replies.content:
As you may be aware, there's a controversy in physics as to whether to treat "wavefunction collapse" as a physically real phenomenon in itself, as opposed to it just being a calculation convenience for the sake of relating to some more obscure process by which the rules of quantum state-evolution push realness around and separate some real classical-universe-perspectives where one thing happened from some other real classical-universe-perspectives where another thing happened, or something like that.

My opinion, as half-presented in the following earlier comment, is that the only reasonable way currently on the table for something like the single-universe picture of wavefunction collapse to be real in itself is if something -- e.g. as represented by Norns, in your imagery -- is using considerations outside of physics to decide that one (or more) timeline(s) should have (more) experiencedness or significantness invested in it(/them) than the others, and if in addition the thing outside physics is also having its "experiencedness criterion" work in a way that's more or less compatible with the regular operations of quantum mechanics. (The alternative would be that the thing-outside-physics was pulling out choices of subjective experience from the quantum-physical states in some other way, that were to "cut against the grain" of how quantum mechanics normally worked.) I'm sorry that comment (and to some extent this one) aren't very well written.

https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/308210.html?thread=52905458

In some ways, the fact that two superposed possibilities on the quantum level resolve during a measurement into two distinguished outcomes on the classical level is no more surprising than the fact that a wave which bounces off a wedge-shaped breakwater separates into two wavefronts. In the analogy, the existence of the apparatus that makes a measurement corresponds to the fact that the initial wave is aimed at the breakwater. The physical structure of the breakwater itself, in the analogy, corresponds to the structure of amounts of required energy associated with the different states passed through during the measurement. The measurement process corresponds to the wave interacting with the breakwater. There is a physical calculation technique that is used to track the quantum process which corresponds to the separation of the wavefronts; the phenomenon which this technique centrally describes is called "decoherence".

Having "wavefunction collapse" be a real thing, in the metaphor, would correspond to having someone pick one of the points in one of the wavefronts, apply some criterion to figure out what other points count as "the same wavefront" as opposed to "a different wavefront", and go in with a huge bucket and scoop up all the water from the crests of the other wavefront and use it to fill in the troughs in the other wavefront, as though it had never existed. Also, whoever was doing this would have to be careful to do so in a way that didn't produce any detectable disturbance in the evolution of the other wavefront. This doesn't really seem possible to do without intelligence: what constitutes "part of the same wavefront" as opposed to "part of a different wavefront" is primarily a question of the subjectivities of the beings encoded by the shape and location of the wavefront, and it would be weird for a blind physical process to be able to make such a distinction reliably. So, in some sense, any physicist who supports the "objective" version of the Copenhagen interpretation, where wavefunction collapse is a physically real phenomenon, is kind of conceding the question of the supernatural from the get-go and just doesn't understand that they're doing so. Even with intelligence, it seems like a little more ham-fisted than one would expect from a being with such power.

I appreciate the way that when you discuss wave-particle duality, you do so in a way where the whole universe would be the particle. That is, in fact, the key to beginning to understand what is going on in the subjective appearance of wave-particle duality seeming to be a thing. If I'd gotten to this comment earlier I would have said more to relate your presentation of Urdh to the Bohmian formulation of quantum mechanics, in which the wavefunction is made of a bunch of universe-particles pushing each other around plus also one of the universe-particles is randomly singled out and labeled "real", even though it can't have the dynamics it does unless all the other universe-particles are also there to push it and each other around.

To correct a confusion about the strictly quantum version of entanglement, as opposed to any supernatural phenomena that might piggyback off some of the same structure: In the many-worlds perspective, entanglement is not a mysterious phenomenon. It's just a relation by which you can control which possible worlds near you end up getting tied off with with which possible worlds near someone else, even though you can't control which possibilities themselves go realized or unrealized. This is why you can use entanglement to share randomly generated secret information but not send messages.

comments.title: Sanity Saving Throw
comments.content: When working with magic, one is typically working with forces which are unseen, difficult to concretely trace, and acknowledgement of their very existence offends the sensibilities of a good deal of the general public. If one were going to work with magic as a solitary practitioner, are there any broad steps one should take to ensure that one stays sane and grounded?

replies.title: Re: Sanity Saving Throw
replies.content:
First, it's important to make sure that your mundane life is in good shape. Occultism is not a refuge for people who can't hack ordinary existence! If your bills aren't getting paid, you're neglecting your health, and so on, those are warning signs. Deal with them, using ordinary mundane methods, before busying yourself with magic. Adequate nutrition, enough sleep, and all the other basic rules apply.

Second, a background in occult philosophy is very helpful, since it gives the student a mental framework to go by. That helps avoid confusion and disorientation.

Finally, regular meditation is essential. The lack of meditation practice is one of the main sources of mental imbalance in magic. Mind you, it can be overdone, but 15-30 minutes of meditation a day, every day, never hurt anyone and helps maintain mental clarity.

replies.title: Re: Sanity Saving Throw
replies.content: I'd also suggest a familiarity with basic psychology, its vocabulary, and how it works. It's been a while since I read it, but Dion Fortune's Psychic Self-Defence has a lot of useful material on this, if you're not already familiar with this book.

comments.title: Kolinahr
comments.content:
Hi Everyone,
I had an interesting experience the other day. I had an emotional attachment to a younger family member that was causing me a lot of turbulent emotions. This person is not a bad person but we do not fit well together. She is a drama queen and enjoyed getting an emotional reaction from me. Some months ago, we stopped all contact but I was still very angry with her. It had to do with her being unsafe and stupid, in my opinion, with her little baby. I felt very protective of the baby.

A few days ago, I opened a grove and performed a ritual that I had devised. I gathered all my emotions for her in the centre of my chest and exhaled them. At first, I perceived the emotions as black smoke and then grey, lighter grey and the ninth and last breath was white. I felt immeasurably better at once and have not had any emotions for her since.

I have also used this technique to rid myself of fibromyalgia pain with great success. I have yet to make it work with a migraine. I have named this technique Kolinahr after the Star Trek Vulcan practice of ridding oneself of all emotions.

I hope some of you will try this to see how it will work for you.

I have also spent a bit over a year meditating on the lessons in the Occult Philosophy Workbook and I feel it has done me good. I plan to meditate on other things for a year and then do all the meditations again.
Maxine

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Ah, the fine art of egregor repair! Though it doesn't actually feel like a fine art when you're in the middle of it -- it's more like pulling a wagon free of the mud...

It's entirely possible to fix a damaged egregor. What you need to do is discuss the matter with your husband and agree that you're going to change the habits in question, and then both apply conscious, sustained efforts to that task. Go to the opposite extreme if you have to. Every time the behavior comes up, catch it, stop it, and reverse it. Yes, it's a grueling experience, but it's the same thing we all have to do when we set out to improve our karma by changing our behavior. Do it, and you can neutralize the collective karma and push the egregor back in a constructive direction.

comments.title: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
comments.content:
My sister, as mentioned hitherto, has gone out of her mind. Her core hallucinations have resolved into an array of consistent themes, notable among them the following. For years she was a member of a fundamentalist sect - one that may be fairly characterized as a cult - and eventually broke with them on bad terms. Now she believes that members of this sect are in the woodwork - not figuratively but literally - or in the next room or down the hall, and that they are influencing events to affect her adversely, and that they are tormenting her with various devices.

This theme has proven consistent enough that I have come to think that maybe it is not all pure delusion. I recall your once expressing the opinion that in some cases prayer by fundamentalist evangelists can constitute a form of malefic magic, specifically when intended to convert or otherwise manipulate other persons. I have begun to suspect that something of this nature may be going on with my sister, that she is being attacked by an egregore in a kind of magico-spiritual vengeance.

If that is the case, is there anything magically that I can do about it?

My magic background is Hellenic Golden Dawn; it’s at a modest level of attainment, but I do have what I believe to be a good Hellenic answer to the Rose Cross ritual. Might that perhaps prove useful in such a case? Or could some other form of magic help? Her condition is dire enough that it even seems to call for something in the nature of an exorcism. If a person experienced in that - Lon Duquette is the one I happen to know about - offered to help with this, I’d take him up on it.

I’m pretty sure my sister’s mind is still there. In those moments nearest lucidity, she knows and remembers all our common references, including literary ones. Though the situation appears hopeless, I do cherish some hope that if she could be freed from such ideations she just might be able to recover herself.

All the medical establishment chooses to do is give her psych meds. They calm her down somewhat, along with all the toxicity and side effects.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content:
Obviously this situation was vastly milder than yours, so may not be applicable, but in case it helps:

I believe that I was being influenced by my in-laws’ (undoubtedly very well-intentioned) prayers to turn to Jesus. They are very good people, also very filled with the Spirit (prophecy, speaking in tongues, etcetera). But what they see as a great gift to me I experienced as intrusive and wrong-feeling. I simply prayed to my own deities that they free my will from any coercive influences so that I would be able to engage with the Divine in the ways that were right for me. I haven’t had any problems or nagging/intrusive thoughts about it since. If you are authorized to pray on your sister’s behalf on this subject, or if she could pray, it might help.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content: You can certainly use an R+C ritual equivalent for this; you'll probably want to do it daily. You might also consider invoking Apollo and Artemis on her behalf, if she's willing to have you do this.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content: Yes, when in her right mind she has on several occasions expressed her willingness for magic to be done on her behalf.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content: Excellent. Then I recommend the ritual work daily, and regular prayers to the deities in question.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content:
I forgot to ask: why Apollo and Artemis, specifically? Is it because they’re brother and sister? I’ve asked my sister who is her favorite goddess, and she responded “Diana.” I imagine Artemis is close enough, as both are moon goddesses.

Some years ago while writhing in a dentist’s chair I seemed to hear Apollo say, “wanna work with me?” Odd time for it, but there it is.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content:
Apollo is one of the gods of healing, and as a sun god (and simply because he is who he is) he's the supreme example of sanity in the ancient Greek pantheon. Artemis, his sister, is the goddess of the Moon, which rules over mental illness. Diana is simply the Roman name for Artemis; you can invoke Apollo and Diana, if you like, just as the Romans did. If your sister feels close to Diana, it's quite possible that you can do something.

Planetary charity to Moon charities -- for example, anything related to birth, very young children, or the sea -- might also be called for.

replies.title: Re: Banishing, exorcism for madness?
replies.content:
Have you tried talking with her about what she thinks/feels/imagines is going on as though she were in fact still completely sane, which she may well be? Perhaps her former sect is thoroughly demon infested. Perhaps they not only gaslight and brainwash their victims, but load them up with metaphysical parasites as well. Perhaps your sister is thinking as lucidly as she can under that pernicious and depleting influence.

Her vocabulary describing her understanding of what she's going through may be quite different from any vocabulary that you or I would employ, but it could be just as consistent and accurate as any that we would use. And let's face it, since we're both writing here at a Magic Monday thread, there's plenty of people who would reflexively brand us crazy and use our strange vocabulary as proof of our insanity!

What if your sister is right and her former cultmates are astrally projecting into nearby rooms or sending their demons to trace out destabilizing patterns in the grain of the woodwork around her? What if she's only metaphorically right and all their brainwashing has left her feeling as though her captors are always nearby, reinforcing their disorienting signal, or that all of the rigid framing and containing structures erected within and around her (the woodwork) have been dangerously infested by her captors? If only the rest of us were so acutely aware of how rigidly boxed in we've become by all of the gaslighting propaganda that's been inflicted on us over the years. What if your sister were going sane as much as going crazy?

That's not to take your or her frustration, fear, and upset lightly. I had a schizophrenic uncle who was frighteningly unstable, yet his worldview became far more interesting and comprehensible to me when I finally realized that he was actually eloquently expressing through metaphor all the hidden truths that my family had so meticulously tried to repress.

The best way to become an acknowledged part of a severely autistic child's world is to copy their rocking, sometimes for months on end, until they eventually allow you to introduce some small change to their pattern and they follow your lead. One small change at a time, you can introduce tapping, then drumming, then drumming with pens (pointilism at its finest), then drawing, then writing, and then you get to find out how brilliant they always were - just disconnected, in their own world with its own vocabulary. It can take months to get sufficiently in sync with their world, their repertoire of self-soothing habits, their unique vocabulary. If you're willing to take someone else's constrained, distorted worldview seriously enough for long enough, they will eventually return the favor and begin paying your world tentative little visits. You might even get to find out how sane they always were - just disconnected.

Your sister may already know exactly what workings could most help her, since she lived through and probably co-participated in the kind of workings that harmed her. Whether that would be group prayer, or casting out of demons, or struggle sessions, or calling down angels, her soul will certainly know what healing it needs even if her conscious mind has been thoroughly befuddled. Listen for the Freudian slips, unnecessary emphases, word reversals, thoughtless ramblings, etc. by which all of our souls manage to get their deep wisdom slipped past our censors. If demons are involved, as sounds likely, know that only half of those slips will be true expressions of her soul, with the other half being the demons' lies and distractions.

- Christophe

comments.title: Grounding
comments.content: Hi JMG, what do you think of the practice of grounding? That is, entering a meditative state and focusing on your connection with the Earth below so as to disperse stray energies. I've heard some sources describe it as a must for following up magical workings and sometimes use during rituals so you don't get "contaminated" but I haven't seen you mention it. Thanks.

replies.title: Re: Grounding
replies.content: It's not something I practice but I know that some people get good results from it.

replies.title: Re: Grounding
replies.content:
So far as I know, grounding and centering both entered modern occultism from Starhawk's very influential book The Spiral Dance back in the 1970s. She was highly influenced by earlier trends in California occultism, especially its New Thought trends, and apparently only became much interested with Witchcraft after meeting Z Budapest.

Centering was laid out as a fundamental New Thought practice by Lida A. Churchill in her book The Magic Seven (1901), chapter 1. (That book is available online at http://iapsop.com/ssoc/1901__churchill___the_magic_seven.pdf and is basically a good account of the fundamentals of New Thought magic -- magic without any serial numbers filed off!) I haven't yet found a similar published source for grounding, but I'm confident there is one out there somewhere, maybe even in one of Churchill's many other books.

comments.title: Druidism originating with Phoenicians?
comments.content:
The Rev. W. Phelps in his The History and Antiquities of Somersetshire mentions that "...Druidism...is supposed to have been introduced into this country....from the East, by the Phoenicians, who held a commercial intercourse with Cornwall.."

He mentioned the writings of Tacitus, Diodorus and Caesar as references but it isn't clear if they made the Phoenician claim or it was later conjectures. The Rev. Phelps then references Godfrey, Higgins and his The Celtic Druids in which "the philosophers of the Phoenicians were the same as the Druids.." along with manners, religion and even language.

Little do I know about Phoenician religion and classic Druidism so this prompts a question as the claim goes beyond a statement such as "Phoenicians traded with the British Isles in order to procure tin and there was some cultural exchange".

Do you find similarities between Phoenician religion and Druidism that would make this claim plausible?

Link to The Celtic Druids .

comments.title: Questions
comments.content:
Good morning JMG and everyone. Thank you for doing MM.

I had a few questions I hoped you might know something about.

1. Is there a generally accepted occult view on fate/destiny vs. epigenesis/free will? It seems like both are real things even though they are contradictory to each other. Magic supports the idea that we can affect the course of our lives, but I realized in meditation that even the Logos is bound by the fate of the Cosmos in the sense that it must work with the cosmic matter that happened to be along its path toward the 7th plane. Is it as simple as there being certains fates that we are bound to no matter what, while other aspects of existence aren't as critical to the overall pattern and can therefore be altered? Are fate and free will in some sense actually a yin-yang type unity rather than opposing polarized?

2. I have come to realize that one of the things I would like to alter about this incarnation is the persistent and often overwhelming self-doubt that seems like a constant life companion sometimes. I was wondering what planets/zodiac signs/deities would be the best to work with to reduce this self doubt. I know the Sun is an obvious answer here, but I would rather not switch to arrogance, rather just reduce the insecurity, but maybe that is a distinction without a difference.

3. What is the resolution to the differences in correspondences between the 4 triplicities and the 12 houses? Something like how spouses fall under the seventh house when looking at the 12, but they correspond to the 4th daughter when looking at the triplicities. Does one of these supercede the other, or is it just a matter of keeping these different interpretive methods separate from each other?

Thank you again for hosting MM

Geoff

replies.title: Re: Questions
replies.content: 1) Good! They are in fact in a yin-yang relationship. As I understand the occult teaching, we are all born with the capacity for free will, but most of us never use it -- we lurch through life doing exactly what we've been programmed to do by our heredity, our culture, and our childhood experiences. Some of us begin to awaken the capacity to will in life. At first it's little more than a bit of wiggle room, but if we keep working at it we can get to the point of being able to exercise genuine choice in at least a few situations. Those choices, in turn, become part of our karmic inheritance in future lives. Thus free will is never absolute -- it's always in a balance with fate (the Western term for karma) and destiny (the pull of the soul toward its ultimate purpose) -- but it can become very, very powerful.

replies.title: Re: Questions

comments.title: church energy gone bad?

replies.title: Re: church energy gone bad?

It was almost certainly the departure of the convent that soured things. comments.title: too foolish not to share
comments.content:
I’m mainly offline just now, but this seems too foolish on my part not to share:

Amusing error and good example of how systems do not always map to each other – I thought Brunton’s Long Path and Short Path were analogous to JMG’s The Two Paths (O P Workbook pages 88 & 89):

“Human spiritual evolution unfolds along two paths. The broad path of everyday life leads eventually to the summit, but it winds from one unbalanced condition to its opposite, and takes many lives to reach its goal. The narrow path of occult study and practice leads straight to the goal and takes far fewer lives. All souls at the human level start out on the broad and winding path, and most remain there, for the narrow path is for the few who feel the call of the great adventure of awakening.”

I was wrong. In Brunton’s model, there seems to be the winding path of general humanity struggling to be human, the long path of preparation for those engaged in methods, activities, disciplines and practices, and then a short path for those who, having developed a spiritual foundation, shift primary focus to the manifestation of higher self/divine where ego is a tool and channel for the higher self into the world.
It’s only taken me nearly three years to realise that!
And that is assuming I have it correct this time… which is open to conjecture!

Now I may understand Brunton’s downer on occult powers; it’s not the activities/abilities per se but that if personality/ego is not developed/purified, the desire for occult powers is likely to be a lower ego-based sport of excitement, thrills and power - DWS - Dogs with Wings Syndrome
Even Barliman Butterbur can see through a brick wall in time.

replies.title: Re: too foolish not to share
replies.content: There are plenty of ways to divide the various options. I tend toward the optimistic belief that sooner or later, no matter how clueless you are, the slow tide of spiritual evolution will get you to your goal, and I contrast that with those who get to work; Brunton is more concerned with differences among the people who get to work. Both are valid distinctions -- and it does take a certain amount of reflection to notice the difference!

replies.content:
Okay, first of all, New Thought is a great example of the law that any belief system taken to its logical extreme ends in absurdity. As I like to point out, you don't create your own reality; you co-create your own reality. There's this little bitty thing called the universe that also has a say in the matter! You can use New Thought methods such as affirmations to help you co-create your reality into a shape more to your liking, but there are limits, and if you try to push past them you will trip over your own feet and bloody your nose.

Too many New Thought types don't realize that, or more precisely refuse to realize it. They've fallen into egocentric modes of thinking in which the whole cosmos revolves around whatever they happen to want, and when the cosmos fails to act accordingly, they either rationalize that away or go in for conspiracy culture so they can find someone to blame. (I've noticed that New Thought believers tend to be early converts to conspiracy notions of the Evil Space Lizards variety.)

So, can you manifest things? Sure, within the limits of the laws of nature, the principles of economics, and your own willingness to work hard and learn from your mistakes. If you're interested, there's a fine New Thought course from the sane and reasonable wing of the movement available for free download here:

http://iapsop.com/archive/materials/wing_lessons/order_of_the_essenes_florida/

The first 23 lessons in particular are worth close study, not least for their graceful balance between New Thought ideas and practical realities. (The author was a successful businessman.) But if you think that you can get the universe to hand you a gold-plated Rolls Royce by doing nothing more than inflating your sense of entitlement to the bursting point, I'm sorry to say the universe is going to teach you a very uncomfortable lesson.

As for scam artists, people who believe that they're entitled to have whatever they want are the Happy Hunting Ground for bunco artists of every description. Every fraud depends on convincing people that they can get something for nothing, and that belief is hardwired into the delusional end of the New Thought movement. (By contrast, teachings like those in the course I linked above are focused on the idea that you can have anything you desire if you are willing to work for it.)

replies.title: Re: Neville Goddard Critics
replies.content:
Israel Regardie had a thoughtful and interesting chapter on Goddard in his book The Romance of Metaphysics. You can find it online pretty easily and I recommend reading it.

Goddard is a fascinating figure because his claims about what is possible are about as extensive as any you’ll find, but he does seem to have been the kind of person who lived his life in exactly the way you’d expect a sincere and morally upright person to live—he was never a guru, he didn’t try to establish his own church or religion, no complicated secrets for sale to elite believers, etc.

His techniques seem to have genuinely worked well for him in part because of the kind of person that he was, but people who get absurd and over-the-top fantasies about what can be accomplished by his methods should consider paying attention to his own accounts of what he did with them, which are pretty consistently within the bounds of what a reasonable person might expect practical magic to do.

replies.title: Re: Neville Goddard Critics
replies.content:
I’ve found Neville Goddard quite helpful. I don’t know if his ideas will take you as far as he said they could, but I’ve successfully dealt with some VERY sticky situations following his precepts.

BTW, after listening to quite a few of his lectures, I think his ideas may be getting misrepresented online. He *never* talks about the Law of Attraction; it’s always the Law of Assumption with him. I can’t recall him using the term “manifestation,” though it’s possible he may have done.

Also, I’ve noticed many fake videos of him on YouTube. They seem to be AI-generated and use a synthetic voice that mimics his, saying things that he may have written or said, or maybe not. The genuine recordings of his lectures have rather low sound quality, as the microphone was (seemingly) not on him.

I’ve noticed the same phenomenon with other figures, notably C.G. Jung. There was one video that presented as his ideas that I’m sure he never expressed. The relevant thumbnail images are also AI generated and look fake. Watch out for phony misleading videos of notable occult and New Thought figures!

replies.title: Re: Neville Goddard Critics
replies.content: For what it's worth, I started a conversation with the critics, and so far they primarily seem to be talking about coaches such as Sammy Ingram who picked up Goddard's ideas and pushed them to the point of absurdity. We'll see how the conversation ends up, but so far it's been productive.

comments.title: Mantra in Western tradition?
comments.content:
I am looking for a simple mantra that I can use while meditating. I would prefer something in the western tradition. Would IAO be a good choice?

What I would like is something that enables me to develop some tranquility (not emptiness!) of mind, for I have been in a far-from-tranquil state for a long time, due to some very difficult and challenging conditions.

Also, it would make me really happy if my practice helped me to develop a closer relationship with my HGA - in a modest, taking-it-fairly-easy way; I’m not ready to take on the Abramelin operation just yet, but I’d like to work gently in that direction.

Incidentally, I’ve always liked the Hare Krishna mantra. But the global Hare Krishna organization ISKAN seem decidedly fundamentalist and they apparently think that everyone ought to be celibate, which strikes me as a really weird position to take for someone who is worshipping a love god. So that all kind of puts me off.

replies.title: Re: Mantra in Western tradition?
replies.content:
IAO is quite traditional as a mantra in Western practice. You can use any other divine name that appeals to you. You can also use any simple declarative sentence. For example:

(breathing in) My Holy Guardian Angel,

(breathing out) I align my will with yours.

Such things have seen a great deal of use in the last century and a half of Western occultism, with very good results.

replies.title: Re: Mantra in Western tradition?
replies.content: Thanks! I think this will work. I’ve tried a few rounds, and it’s good so far: very good.

comments.title: nightmares and such
comments.content:
As a kid, I used to have crazy nightmares, and I've gathered that this is a common childhood experience for others too. I'm wondering, what is it about one's early years that puts you in touch with the lower or mid-lower astral plane? I gather that the worst nightmares are lower astral, while other bad dreams are at the lower end of the middle astral, if I'm not mistaken.

Related to the above, why is it such a bad idea to gaze into mirrors in dreams? I can confirm this usually does not end well, and discussions I've seen on /x/ confirm it's not just me. What is it with astral mirrors?

replies.title: Re: nightmares and such
replies.content:
1) Children are much more sensitive to their astral surroundings than most adults. If those surroundings are grubby -- and that's normal these days, since basic spiritual hygiene has been completely forgotten by most people -- dreams are going to be pretty bad.

2) Hmm! That's not one I've encountered before. Come to think of it, though, I don't think I've ever encountered a mirror in my dreams.

replies.title: Re: nightmares and such
replies.content:
I dunno what causes it, but mine were relentless and overwhelming when I was a kid. Found out as an adult that the rental house next to us had been occupied by a whole series of awful people, including a murderer dodging an arrest warrant, a family with neglected children who tormented animals, a seriously OCD fellow who'd run his leaf-blower at all hours of the day and night, and a dude who later got arrested for voyeurism. Reckon the house itself had some bad juju going on, as renting or purchasing, nobody ever seems to stay there for more than three years. Wish somebody'd bulldoze it.

Heckuva place to live next to. Still amazes me that nightmares were the worst effect.

replies.title: Re: nightmares and such
replies.content:
I see, thanks!

And yea, that house sounds like bad news, must be alot of negative energy there. I remember watching urban exploration on youtube where doods were wandering through an abandoned prison complex, and even though they weren't into the occult, they mentioned the harsh, despairing energy of their surroundings. So it seems like negative astral energy can seep into the floorboards, quite literally.

There was one case where looking into an astral mirror went okay -- it was the one where I was seemingly astral traveling in my house, and an entity seized me and caused me to float around; when I resisted, it leaned me into the bathroom mirror, where I saw a much younger version of myself before waking up. While being controlled by an entity was objectionable, the mirror part was strange but not unpleasant, and that makes it exceptional. Other times my appearance would be mutated horribly, among other effects.

replies.title: Re: nightmares and such
replies.content: The WWII era open bay barracks I had to live in in the USAF had a really bad vibe.  I mean, expressly the ones at Lowry AFB in Aurora, Colorado.  They are no longer there.  After Lowry AFB closed, that piece of land was turned into apartments, now notorious for Venezuelan gang presence.

replies.title: Re: mirrors in dreams
replies.content:
In my case, it was like this: I saw myself in a full length mirror, and the person I found there wasn’t me. He was my doppelgänger. And he wanted to kill me. He came out of the mirror, and I had to run or fight for my life. (I think I ran - his strength was equal to or greater than mine, and his intention murderous.) This dream occurred a couple of times.

This was thirty years ago or more, but it was very memorable and I haven’t forgotten. I have no idea what it was about, except that apparently my alter ego wasn’t too happy with me.

comments.title: Internal Quiet

Dr. Greer --

For the past two months, nearly three, I have found myself noticing a certain quiet within myself. Becoming angry about anything is rare -- I cannot get myself angry, or remain angry, for very long, and when I have gotten upset, it is usually due to something directly in front of me, related to food or shelter. Other things are mildly irritating, perhaps, but I find them more laughable or "telling" than anything else. I am not floating outside of my body or the physical world, but I do feel like I am watching things going on around me, and not feeling especially bothered by ones I know I am unable to change. The translation into words would be something like "Well, that is happening.". I do not know if this is peace, good sense, or something more dangerous.
